perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be a contract under the State of Minnesota and for all purposes shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of Minnesota, excluding any choice of law provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall generally be construed under the laws of Minnesota, but other jurisdictions may apply.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity by stating the agreement is 'generally' construed under Minnesota law, but allows for the possibility of other jurisdictions applying. This creates uncertainty and could lead to disputes over which state's laws govern the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-301(a) allows parties to agree that the law of a particular state will govern their rights and duties, but the modified text undermines this by suggesting other jurisdictions might apply. This creates ambiguity and makes the governing law unclear, potentially conflicting with the principle of contractual autonomy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-301 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-301 336.1-209 336.1-302 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-301 Version List Recent History 2024 336.1-301 Amended 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 2004 336.1-301 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 336.1-301 TERRITORIAL APPLICABILITY; PARTIES' POWER TO CHOOSE APPLICABLE LAW. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) section 336.2-402 ; (2) sections 336.2A-105 and 336.2A-106 ; (3) section 336.4-102 ; (4) section 336.4A-507 ; (5) section 336.5-116 ; (6) section 336.8-110 ; (7) sections 336.9-301 through 336.9-307 ; (8) section 336.12-107 . History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 16 ; 2024 c 93 art 1 s 3 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity regarding which jurisdiction's laws apply, contradicting the principle outlined in Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-301(a). The original text clearly designates Minnesota law, while the changed text allows for the possibility of other jurisdictions applying. The Perturbation Explanation highlights the uncertainty created. The Law Explanation supports that parties may agree that the law of a particular state will govern their rights. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this point, stating: '(a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties.' By adding 'generally' and allowing for 'other jurisdictions,' the changed text weakens the original agreement on Minnesota law, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall be permitted to: (a) disclose Confidential Information to its officers, employees and counsel, but only to the extent reasonably necessary in order for such party to prepare, conduct and execute and deliver definitive documents for the Transaction; provided that Receiving Party shall take all such action as shall be necessary or desirable in order to ensure that each of such persons maintains the confidentiality of any Confidential Information that is so disclosed; and (b) disclose Confidential Information to the extent, but only to the extent, required by law; provided, that prior to making any disclosure pursuant to this subsection, the Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party of the same, and the Disclosing Party shall have the right to participate with the Receiving Party in determining the amount and type of Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, if any, which must be disclosed in order to comply with applicable law, including, without limitation, state and federal securities laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall be permitted to disclose Confidential Information to its officers, employees and counsel, as needed. Additionally, Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information if required by law, informing Disclosing Party when practical.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality. Removing the 'reasonably necessary' and 'all such action as shall be necessary or desirable in order to ensure' phrases introduces substantial ambiguity. The phrase 'as needed' is vague and open to interpretation. Replacing the mandatory notification with 'informing Disclosing Party when practical' makes the notification process discretionary, potentially allowing for disclosures without proper consultation.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 325E.61, the Minnesota Trade Secrets Act, protects trade secrets by imposing a duty to maintain their confidentiality. The original text provided a reasonable level of assurance in maintaining confidentiality, while the modified text weakens these protections through ambiguous language, potentially allowing for unauthorized disclosures and therefore contradicting the spirit and intent of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 325E.61 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 325E Section 325E.61 325E.60 325E.63 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 325E Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 325E.61 Version List Recent History 2006 Subd. 1 Amended 2006 c 233 s 1 2006 Subd. 4 Amended 2006 c 233 s 1 2006 Subd. 5 Amended 2006 c 212 art 1 s 17 2005 325E.61 New 2005 c 167 s 1 325E.61 DATA WAREHOUSES; NOTICE REQUIRED FOR CERTAIN DISCLOSURES. § Subdivision 1. Disclosure of personal information; notice required. (a) Any person or business that conducts business in this state, and that owns or licenses data that includes personal information, shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of this state whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person. The disclosure must be made in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay, consistent with the legitimate needs of law enforcement, as provided in paragraph (c), or with any measures necessary to determine the scope of the breach, identify the individuals affected, and restore the reasonable integrity of the data system. (b) Any person or business that maintains data that includes personal information that the person or business does not own shall notify the owner or licensee of the information of any breach of the security of the data immediately following discovery, if the personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthori
perturbation.justification: The original contract language provides specific obligations to maintain confidentiality, while the changed text weakens these obligations and introduces ambiguity. Specifically, the change removes the requirement that disclosures be 'reasonably necessary' and that the Receiving Party 'take all such action as shall be necessary or desirable in order to ensure' confidentiality. The phrase 'as needed' is also vague. The change also makes the notification process discretionary. According to the LLM explanation, the changes contradict the intent of Minnesota Statutes § 325E.61, which aims to protect trade secrets by imposing a duty to maintain their confidentiality. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to data breaches and the disclosure of personal information, mandating notice to residents whose unencrypted personal information was acquired by an unauthorized person. Although the snippet is about data breaches and not trade secrets directly, it does show Minnesota's commitment to protecting sensitive information, which is undermined by the weakening of confidentiality obligations in the contract.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges and agrees that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay would be irreparably damaged in the event that any of the provisions of this Agreement are not performed by the Receiving Party in accordance with their specific terms or are otherwise breached. Accordingly, it is agreed that the Disclosing Party or GreenWay shall be entitled to an injunction or injunctions to prevent breaches of this Agreement by the Receiving Party and shall have the right to specifically enforce this Agreement and the terms and provisions hereof against the Receiving Party in addition to any other remedy to which the Disclosing Party or GreenWay may be entitled in law or equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that the Disclosing Party and GreenWay may experience damages if provisions of this Agreement are not generally followed. The Disclosing Party or GreenWay may seek remedies as they deem appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause clearly states that a breach would cause irreparable damage and that the Disclosing Party is entitled to an injunction. The modified text replaces 'irreparably damaged' with 'may experience damages', weakening the acknowledgement of harm. It also replaces the entitlement to an injunction and specific enforcement with 'may seek remedies as they deem appropriate,' which is vague and does not guarantee access to equitable relief.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 65 governs the issuance of injunctions. While it doesn't guarantee an injunction in every case, the original contract language acknowledges the potential for irreparable harm, which is a key element in obtaining injunctive relief. By making the availability of remedies discretionary ('may seek remedies as they deem appropriate'), the modified text makes it more difficult to obtain an injunction, potentially contradicting the purpose of Rule 65, which is to provide a mechanism for equitable relief in appropriate circumstances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original contract's acknowledgment of potential irreparable harm and replaces the entitlement to an injunction with discretionary remedies, creating a contradiction. Rule 65 (a)(1) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, addresses 'Injunctions and Restraining Orders,' indicating the legal system's provision for such remedies. By weakening the clause related to irreparable harm and making remedies discretionary, the modified contract undermines the basis for seeking an injunction, moving away from the specific protections the original language provided, thus resulting in a clear contradiction of established legal procedure.

perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall continue for a reasonable duration.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies that the agreement continues 'indefinitely,' creating a clear and permanent obligation. The modified text replaces this with 'for a reasonable duration,' which is vague and subjective. What constitutes a 'reasonable duration' is open to interpretation and could lead to disputes about when the agreement terminates.
perturbation.law_explanation: Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-309(2) addresses contract duration and termination, implying contracts should have a definite term unless otherwise agreed. While this section technically applies to contracts for the sale of goods, the principle of having a reasonably ascertainable duration is relevant to contract law generally. The change to 'reasonable duration' introduces uncertainty which is contrary to the principle of defining the terms of agreement, and making the terms of agreement concrete.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.2-309 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.2-309 336.2-308 336.2-310 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.2-309 Version List 336.2-309 ABSENCE OF SPECIFIC TIME PROVISIONS; NOTICE OF TERMINATION. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. History: 1965 c 811 s 336 .2-309 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The original contract states an 'indefinitely' duration, while the changed contract states 'reasonable duration.' The Perturbation Explanation clarifies that 'reasonable duration' is vague and subjective, leading to potential disputes. The Law Explanation points to Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-309(2), which implies contracts should have a definite term unless otherwise agreed. Scraped Law Snippet 1, quoting Minnesota Statutes § 336.2-309, states that contracts indefinite in duration are valid for a reasonable time but may be terminated at any time. The changed text introduces uncertainty, undermining the principle of defined terms. The change contradicts the goal of clarity and predictability in contract duration, creating a contradiction by introducing ambiguity where there was none, despite Snippet 1 validating a 'reasonable time' duration. The original 'indefinitely' duration implies permanence. The change removes this, weakening the agreement and opening it to interpretation, satisfying the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.
